title: Armed Forces Retirement Home
content:
The Armed Forces Retirement Home (AFRH) is a facility for retired members of the U.S. military, providing residences and services at two campuses located in Gulfport, Mississippi, and Washington, D.C. It offers a supportive environment with amenities like meals, medical care, and recreational activities for eligible veterans.

The AFRH is funded in part by mandatory payroll deductions from all active duty enlisted personnel, warrant officers, and limited duty officers. This deduction, often a small amount each month, is automatically withheld from military pay and is listed as a separate line item on the Leave and Earnings Statement (LES). The purpose of this payment is to ensure that the AFRH can continue to provide quality care and support for retired service members who may need assisted living or long-term care in the future. By contributing to the AFRH fund, every service member helps maintain a safety net for themselves and their fellow military personnel, ensuring that those who have served have access to dignified retirement living and essential services if needed.

title: Allotments
content:
Allotments are a way for military members to send a portion of their pay directly to a bank account, credit union, or other financial institution. This can be a useful tool for budgeting and saving, as it allows service members to set aside money for specific purposes, such as paying bills or saving for a future goal.

Allotments are essentially automatic payroll deductions that are directed toward a designated recipient or purpose. Military members use allotments to manage their finances efficiently, ensure timely payments, and support family members or causes important to them. Allotments help reduce the risk of missed or late payments and can simplify financial planning, especially during deployments or frequent moves.

There are several types of allotments available to service members:

  • Bank Account Allotment: Directs a portion of pay to a checking or savings account, either for the member or a family member.
  • Discretionary Allotment: Used for purposes chosen by the service member, such as paying rent, loans, or supporting dependents.
  • Insurance Allotment: Pays premiums for life insurance policies, such as Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance (SGLI) or other private insurance plans.
  • Bond Allotment: Used to purchase U.S. savings bonds (though this option is less common today).
  • TRICARE Dental Allotment: Deducts premiums for the TRICARE Dental Program, providing dental coverage for service members and their families.
  • Fundraising Allotments: Allows members to contribute regularly to charitable organizations through payroll deduction. Examples include the Combined Federal Campaign (CFC), Air Force Assistance Fund, Army Emergency Relief, and Navy-Marine Corps Relief Society.

Allotments can be started, changed, or stopped through the service member's payroll or finance office. Some allotments are limited in number or amount, and certain types (such as charitable contributions) may have specific eligibility requirements.

title: Basic Allowance for Housing
content: BAH provides uniformed service members equitable housing compensation based on housing costs in local civilian housing markets within the 50 U.S. states when government quarters are not provided. BAH is not intended to cover all of a service member's housing costs. The opportunity for Service members to choose their off-base housing is important to DoD. Each member has the freedom to decide how to allocate their income (including the housing allowance) without a penalty for deciding to conserve some dollars on rent to pay other expenses. Therefore, actual out-of-pocket expense for an individual may be higher or lower than the prescribed rate based on choice of housing. Allowances reflect the costs associated with household rental. Generally, rental prices change between 2%-5% from year to year. Housing allowances typically change accordingly.

title: Basic Allowance for Subsistence (BAS)
content:
BAS is meant to offset the cost of food for service members. This allowance is based on the historic origins of the military in which the military provided room and board (or rations) as part of a member's pay. This allowance is not intended to offset the costs of meals for family members. Beginning on January 1, 2002, all enlisted members get full BAS, but pay for their meals (including those provided by the government). This is the culmination of the BAS Reform transition period. Since BAS is intended to defray a portion of the cost of food for the service member, its level is linked to the USDA's food cost index. Therefore, each year the BAS rate is adjusted based upon the percentage increase of the price of food as identified by the USDA. This is why the increase to BAS will not necessarily be the same percentage as that applied to the increase in the pay table, as annual pay raises are linked to the increase of private sector wages.

OfficersEnlistedEnlisted II*
$328.48$476.95$953.90

*BAS II is the monthly rate that may be payable to enlisted members on duty at a permanent station and assigned to single (unaccompanied) Government quarters, which do not have adequate food storage or preparation facilities, and where a Government mess is not available, and the Government cannot otherwise make meals available. The BAS II rate is twice the rate of standard enlisted BAS.

title: Base Pay
content:
Base Pay is the fundamental component of U.S. military pay. All members receive it and typically is the largest component of a member's pay. A member's grade and years of service determines the amount of basic pay received.

Base pay is determined by a pay table published annually by the Department of War. As members gain more years of service, their base pay increases at set intervals, rewarding longevity and experience.

Base pay is taxable and forms the foundation for calculating other benefits, such as retirement pay and certain allowances. It does not include additional forms of compensation like Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH), Basic Allowance for Subsistence (BAS), special pays, or bonuses. The pay tables are adjusted periodically to account for inflation and changes in federal law.

Base pay is paid on a monthly basis, typically split into two payments each month if the member elected for mid-month pay.

title: Bonus Pay
content:
Bonus pay in the U.S. military refers to special, one-time or recurring payments made to service members as an incentive for enlisting, reenlisting, or remaining in critical career fields. These bonuses are designed to attract and retain personnel in roles that are essential to military readiness or are difficult to fill due to specialized skills or high demand.

Bonuses are paid out for a variety of reasons, including:

  • Encouraging enlistment in the military, especially for hard-to-fill jobs or during periods of increased need.
  • Retaining experienced service members in critical specialties or career fields.
  • Rewarding members who agree to extend their service commitment for multiple years.

Examples of military bonus pays include:

  • Aviation Continuation Pay (ACP): Offered to qualified aviators who agree to remain on active duty for a specified period.
  • Critical Skills Retention Bonus (CSRB): Paid to service members in high-demand specialties, such as cyber operations or medical fields.
  • Multiyear Retention Bonus (MRB): Provided to members who commit to serve additional years in certain career fields, such as medical or special operations.
  • Nuclear Career Annual Incentive Bonus: Awarded to officers and enlisted personnel in nuclear-related positions to retain their expertise.
  • Selective Enlistment Bonus (SEB): Given to new recruits who enlist in critical specialties or agree to serve in specific locations.
  • Selective Reenlistment Bonus (SRB): Paid to current service members who reenlist in critical specialties or at times of high need.
  • Language Proficiency Bonus: For members who demonstrate proficiency in languages critical to national security.
  • Special Duty Assignment Pay: For members who take on challenging or high-responsibility assignments.

The amount and eligibility criteria for each bonus vary by service branch, specialty, and current military needs. Some bonuses are paid as lump sums, while others are distributed in installments over the service commitment period. All bonus payments are subject to federal income tax unless earned in a designated combat zone.

title: Clothing Allowance
content:
Clothing allowances may be issued to help members pay for their uniforms. There are four main types of clothing allowance: Initial, Replacement, Maintenance, and Extra.

Both officers and enlisted members of the U.S. Armed Forces are entitled to an initial clothing allowance. This allowance is provided to enlisted members upon initial enlistment or upon special qualification. Officers, however, are only entitled to the allowance once except in the situation noted below:

  • Upon first reporting for active duty (other than for training) for a period of more than 90 days.
  • Upon completing at least 14 days of active duty or active duty for training as a member of a Reserve Component
  • Upon completing 14 periods of inactive-duty training as a member of the Ready Reserve (Each period must be of at least 2 hours duration)
  • Upon reporting for the first period of active duty required of a member of the Armed Forces Health Professions Scholarship Program.

Cash Clothing Replacement Allowance is payable only to enlisted members annually following initial clothing allowance on the anniversary month. This is for the replacement of uniforms based on normal wear and tear.

Extra Clothing Allowance are additional to the other two and do not affect them. These allowances are for situations in which a member may need additional uniforms or is required to have civilian clothing to perform their duties.

Military Clothing Maintenance Allowance is for replacement and maintenance of military items during and after three years of active duty. If a member has a break in military service, he/she will start over with the initial clothing allowance upon returning to the service. They will then receive Cash Clothing Replacement Allowance and Military Clothing Maintenance Allowance as applicable.

title: Cost of Living Allowance (COLA)
content:
Service members serving outside of the continental United States (OCONUS) where the cost of living is higher than CONUS receive an Overseas Cost-of-Living Allowance (COLA). Overseas COLA is a non-taxable allowance designed to offset higher prices of non-housing goods and services OCONUS and equalize purchasing power with members stationed in CONUS. It does not reimburse expenses, compensate for remoteness, hardship, loss of spousal income, or non-availabilityof goods and services.

OCONUS COLA is not a fixed amount and should not be considered in household budgeting. Fixed expenses, such as car payments, should be based on what a Service member can afford without the allowance. Overseas COLA can fluctuate based on the exchange rate, as COLA is paid in U.S dollars, but some expenses are typically paid in foreign currency.

title: Combat Pay
content:
A member of a uniformed service may be paid special pay at the rate of $225 per month for any month in which he/she was entitled to basic pay. A member is entitled to IDP when assigned to a designated IDP area. To see which areas are designated as IDP areas, view figure 10-1 in the DOD Financial Management Regulation.

The commander determines HFP based on whether a member is:

  • Subject to hostile fire or mine explosions
  • In an area near hostile fire or mine explosions which endanger the member
  • Killed, injured, or wounded by hostile fire, mines, or any hostile action

title: Debt and Advance Debt
content:
Typically, a debt is caused by an exchange in entitlements; for example the difference in BAH rates from the old PDS and new PDS, and also members receiving table rate BAH when they've been assigned a dorm. The size of the debt is determined by the arrival date on station and the in-processing date with Finance. The wider the gap between those dates, the more time the debt has to accrue.

Options for a debt repayment include a check paid to DFAS, payroll deduction, or filing a waiver or a remission. If filing a remission, a completed package must be turned in within 30 days from notification in order to suspend the debt from collection.

Advance debt notifies the member that they've been overpaid. This usually occurs during a PCS or a deployment, and the overpayment occurred in a previous months pay cycle. Members have 30 days to contact your finance office to inquire about possible repayment options. If no action is taken the debt will be recouped in full the following pay period*.

*Debt has not been satisfied until it shows up in the deductions column of your LES if paid by payroll deduction.

title: Federal Taxes
content:
All U.S. citizens, including U.S. military service members, are required to pay federal income tax. Federal taxes are collected by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) and fund government operations, national defense, infrastructure, and public services. Service members pay tax as a percentage of their income, which is calculated using a system of tax brackets. As income increases, the rate applied to each additional portion of income also increases, but only the income within each bracket is taxed at that bracket's rate.

Military pay that is subject to federal tax includes base pay, bonuses, and most special pays. However, some allowances, such as Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) and Basic Allowance for Subsistence (BAS), are not taxable. Additionally, pay earned while serving in a designated combat zone is excluded from federal income tax for enlisted members and warrant officers, and partially excluded for officers.

Tax brackets are adjusted annually and are based on filing status (single, married filing jointly, etc.). Service members may also be eligible for certain tax credits and deductions, such as the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC), Child Tax Credit, and deductions for moving expenses related to Permanent Change of Station (PCS) moves. The IRS also provides special extensions and benefits for those deployed to combat zones or serving overseas.

It's important for service members to review their Leave and Earnings Statement (LES) to ensure the correct amount of federal tax is being withheld. Members can adjust their withholding by submitting a new IRS Form W-4 through their payroll office. Proper tax planning can help avoid owing taxes at the end of the year or receiving an unexpectedly large refund.

For more information, service members should consult the IRS or their installation's finance office, as military tax situations can be complex and may change based on duty location, deployment status, and other factors.

resources.title: IRS Federal Tax Information for Military
IRS Eligibility for Military Tax Benefits
Military OneSource MilTax Free Filing Software

title: Federal Insurance Contributions Act
content: FICA is a U.S. federal payroll tax that is automatically deducted from an employee's paycheck. Taxes under FICA are composed of the old-age, survivors, and disability insurance taxes, also known as Social Security taxes, and the hospital insurance taxes, also known as Medicare taxes. FICA generates money for Social Security and Medicare programs through taxes imposed on nearly every employee and employer in the United States.

title: Hardship Duty Pay
content:
Hardship Duty Pay (HDP) is a special pay authorized for U.S. military service members assigned to duty locations or missions where living conditions are significantly below those in the continental United States, or where the nature of the assignment imposes additional hardship. HDP is intended to compensate for the difficult living conditions, involuntary extensions, or demanding missions, and to encourage members to serve in these challenging environments.

There are several types of Hardship Duty Pay:

  • HDP-Location (HDP-L): Paid to members assigned to designated locations where living conditions are substantially below U.S. standards. Examples include remote overseas bases or areas with limited infrastructure, amenities, or services.
  • HDP-Mission (HDP-M): Provided to members performing particularly demanding missions, such as peacekeeping, humanitarian, or other operations that involve significant personal hardship or risk, regardless of location.
  • HDP-Involuntary Extension (HDP-IE): Paid to members who are involuntarily extended in their assignments or deployments beyond the normal period, often due to operational requirements or stop-loss policies.

The amount of HDP varies based on the type and severity of the hardship, and is determined by the Department of War. HDP is paid in addition to other entitlements, such as base pay and allowances, and is taxable. Eligibility and payment rates are subject to change based on current military needs and the specific assignment or location.

title: Incentive Pay
content:
Incentive Pay is additional compensation provided to U.S. military service members who perform duties that require special skills, involve hazardous conditions, or demand significant responsibility beyond normal assignments. These pays are designed to attract and retain personnel in critical roles, reward specialized expertise, and compensate for increased risk or unique job requirements. Incentive pays are separate from base pay, allowances, and specialty pays, and eligibility is determined by specific qualifications, certifications, or assignments.

Some common types of incentive pay include:

  • Assignment Incentive Pay (AIP): Offered to service members who accept assignments in locations or roles that are difficult to fill or require extended tours, such as remote overseas postings or high-demand specialties.
  • Aviation Career Incentive Pay (ACIP): Also known as flight pay, this is provided to qualified pilots and flight officers who perform regular flight duties and meet career aviation service requirements.
  • Career Enlisted Flyer Incentive Pay (CEFIP): Paid to enlisted aircrew members who perform in-flight duties as part of their career field.
  • Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay (HDIP): Awarded to members who perform hazardous duties, such as parachute jumping (jump pay), demolition, handling toxic fuels, or working in high-risk environments.
  • High Altitude/Low Altitude Pay: Provided to members who participate in high-altitude or low-altitude flight operations, often in challenging or dangerous conditions.
  • Incentive Special Pay: Includes various pays for unique skills or assignments, such as submarine duty, diving, or working with certain types of equipment or technology.

The amount and eligibility criteria for each type of incentive pay vary by service branch, specialty, and current military needs. Some incentive pays are paid monthly, while others may be paid as lump sums or annual bonuses. Service members should consult their personnel or finance office for current eligibility requirements and payment rates.

title: Leave
content:
As part of the military pay and benefits package, military service members earn 30 days of paid leave per year. Service members start at zero and for every month of military service, 2.5 days of leave get added to your leave account. It doesn't stop, but the most members can carry over from one fiscal year to the next fiscal year is 60 days, except in certain, very limited situations where you can carry over more.

Reserve component members, including National Guard, also accrue leave at the rate of 2.5 days for each month that they are on active-duty orders. Reserve components have some special rules for how and when they can use their leave.

Service members are expected to use leave for any workday that they will not be available for work, as required by their command. They are also expected to use leave for any day that they leave the vicinity of their duty station, as defined by their command.

title: Montgomery GI Bill
content:
The Montgomery GI Bill (MGIB) is an education benefit program that provides financial assistance to eligible service members and veterans who wish to pursue higher education, vocational training, or certain certification programs. The MGIB is designed to help cover the cost of tuition, fees, books, and other educational expenses.

To be eligible, service members must generally have completed a minimum service obligation, possess a high school diploma or equivalent, and have contributed $100 per month for the first 12 months of active duty. Benefits are typically available for up to 36 months of full-time education and must be used within 10 years of separation from active duty.

The MGIB can be used for a variety of educational pursuits, including:

  • College degrees (associate, bachelor's, master's)
  • Technical or vocational courses
  • Flight training
  • Apprenticeships and on-the-job training
  • Correspondence courses
  • Licensing and certification tests

Benefit amounts are determined by the length of service, type of education or training, and whether the service member participates in additional programs such as the $600 Buy-Up program, which allows members to increase their monthly benefit by making additional contributions while on active duty.

The MGIB is separate from the Post-9/11 GI Bill, and service members should carefully compare benefits before making an irrevocable election. For more information, visit the VA website or consult your education office.

title: Permanent Change of Station (PCS)
content:
A Permanent Change of Station (PCS) is an official relocation of a U.S. military service member—and often their family—from one duty station to another. PCS moves are a routine part of military life and can occur within the continental United States (CONUS) or to overseas locations (OCONUS). PCS orders are issued for a variety of reasons, including new assignments, promotions, or the completion of a tour of duty.

PCS moves involve a range of entitlements and benefits to help offset the costs and challenges of relocating. These may include:

  • Travel Allowances: Reimbursement for transportation costs for the service member and authorized dependents, including airfare, mileage, and per diem for meals and lodging during travel.
  • Dislocation Allowance (DLA): A one-time payment to help cover miscellaneous moving expenses not otherwise reimbursed.
  • Household Goods Shipment: The government pays to move a set weight allowance of household goods. Members may also be eligible for storage of goods before, during, or after the move.
  • Temporary Lodging Expense (TLE): Reimbursement for temporary housing costs incurred while waiting for permanent housing at the new duty station (CONUS only).
  • Temporary Lodging Allowance (TLA): For OCONUS moves, this helps cover temporary housing costs overseas.
  • Advance Pay: Service members may request advance pay to help cover upfront moving costs, which is then repaid over time.

PCS moves require careful planning. Service members should review their orders, consult with their installation's transportation and finance offices, and use resources like the Defense Personal Property System (DPS) to arrange shipments. It's important to keep receipts and documentation for all expenses related to the move.

PCS moves can also impact state tax residency, entitlements, and benefits eligibility. Spouses and dependents may need to transfer schools, update healthcare enrollment, and adjust to new communities. Support services are available on most installations to assist with these transitions.

title: Pay Entry Base Date (PEBD)
content: The Military Pay Entry Base Date (PEBD) is the date that determines when a service member's active or inactive service counts toward basic pay. It's the date that the military uses to calculate longevity pay, which is an increase in pay based on years of service. While often coinciding with your initial entry into military service, certain factors, like prior service, can adjust this date. A lower PEBD means more years of creditable service, leading to higher pay grades and increased benefits over time.

resources.title: Understanding Your Military Pay Entry Base Date

title: Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance
content:
Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance (SGLI) is a low-cost group term life insurance program available to eligible members of the U.S. military. This includes members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard and certain commissioned members of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and the U.S. Public Health Service (USPHS).

Eligible service members are automatically enrolled in SGLI and issued the maximum coverage amount, which is currently $500,000, unless they choose to reduce or decline coverage. Premiums are automatically deducted from the service member's pay each month. SGLI also offers coverage for traumatic injuries (TSGLI), which provides financial assistance to help service members recover from serious injuries.

Key features of SGLI include:

  • Coverage Amounts: Can be elected in increments of $50,000, up to the maximum of $500,000.
  • Beneficiaries: Service members can designate one or more beneficiaries to receive the insurance payout in the event of their death.
  • Portability: Upon separation from service, members can convert their SGLI coverage to Veterans' Group Life Insurance (VGLI) or a commercial life insurance policy without proof of good health if done within a certain time frame.
  • Family Coverage: Family SGLI (FSGLI) provides coverage for spouses and dependent children at additional cost.
  • Traumatic Injury Protection (TSGLI): Automatically included with SGLI, TSGLI provides short-term financial assistance to help severely injured service members and their families.

SGLI is designed to provide peace of mind and financial security for service members and their families in the event of death or serious injury. Service members can manage their SGLI coverage and beneficiaries through the milConnect website or their personnel office.

title: State Taxes and Home of Record
content:
Home of record is the state or territory where a service member joins the military. It generally remains unchanged unless the individual takes specific actions to change it, and dictates where state taxes must be filed. The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) allows military personnel to maintain their tax residency in their home state even when stationed elsewhere, which can be advantageous if the home state has no income tax. For example, a service member stationed in California but with a home of record in Nevada may avoid California state income taxes if they do not establish residency there. Similarly, military spouses may benefit from the Military Spouses Residency Relief Act, which lets them retain their original state of residency for tax purposes, even after relocating due to military orders.

Receiving Permanent Change of Station (PCS) orders provides an opportunity to reassess tax residency. This can have significant financial implications, especially when moving from a high-tax state to one with lower or no income tax. To change tax residency you must consult your local finance office, which may require certain criteria such as physical presence, intent to remain indefinitely, and actions like obtaining a driver's license or registering to vote in the new state.

title: Specialty Pay
content:
Specialty Pay, also known as Special and Incentive (S&I) Pay, is additional compensation provided to U.S. military service members who possess unique skills, perform hazardous duties, or serve in demanding assignments that go beyond the scope of standard military duties. These pays are designed to attract and retain personnel in critical roles, reward specialized expertise, and compensate for increased risk or responsibility.

Specialty pays are separate from base pay and allowances, and eligibility is determined by specific qualifications, certifications, or assignments. The amount and type of specialty pay can vary by service branch, career field, and current military needs. Some specialty pays are paid monthly, while others may be paid as lump sums or annual bonuses.

Examples of specialty pays include:

  • Foreign Language Proficiency Bonus (FLPB): For members who demonstrate proficiency in languages critical to military operations and national security.
  • Foreign Duty Pay: For service members assigned to duty stations outside the continental U.S. in designated locations.
  • Flight Pay (Aviation Incentive Pay): For pilots, flight officers, and aircrew members who perform regular flight duties.
  • Dive Pay: For qualified divers who perform underwater operations as part of their duties.
  • Jump Pay (Parachute Duty Pay): For members required to perform parachute jumps as part of their assignment, such as airborne or special operations forces.
  • Multiyear Special Pay: For medical, dental, or other professionals who agree to serve additional years in critical specialties.
  • Special Duty Assignment Pay (SDAP): For members serving in particularly demanding or high-responsibility assignments, such as drill instructors or recruiters.
  • Board Certified Pay: For medical officers who achieve board certification in their specialty.
  • Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay: For duties involving significant risk, such as demolition, handling explosives, or working with toxic fuels.

Specialty pays are subject to change based on legislation, Department of Defense policy, and operational requirements. Service members should consult their personnel or finance office for current eligibility criteria and payment rates.

title: Thrift Savings Plan (TSP)
content:
Your decision about Roth and traditional TSP is a choice of when you pay income tax on your TSP contributions and earnings. You can pay taxes either when you earn and contribute the money or when you take it out. There isn't a set formula to determine which choice is best for everyone, and your decision may change as your income and needs change over time. Here are a few questions you'll want to ask yourself to get started:

  • Do I expect a higher or lower income tax rate in retirement?
  • How much money do I need in my paycheck right now?
  • Will my choice of tax treatment affect how much income tax I need to pay?

With Roth TSP, your contributions go into the TSP after tax withholding. That means you pay taxes on your contributions at your current income tax rate. The advantage of the Roth TSP is that you won't pay taxes later when you take out your contributions and any qualified earnings. Earnings are considered qualified after both of these Internal Revenue Code (IRC) requirements are met: 5 years have passed since January 1 of the calendar year when you made your first Roth TSP contribution and you are at least age 59½, permanently disabled, or deceased. Also, your Roth balance isn't subject to IRS required minimum distributions (RMDs), which means you can keep your Roth contributions and earnings in your TSP account as long as you want.

With traditional TSP, your contributions go into the TSP before tax withholding, which can potentially lower your current income tax rate. But when you take money from your traditional TSP, you'll pay taxes on both your contributions and earnings at the income tax rate of the year you make the withdrawal.

You may consider splitting your contributions between Roth and traditional. You can change your contribution type through your agency or service payroll system. Note that if you receive automatic or matching contributions from your agency or service, those contributions will always go into your traditional TSP balance and cannot be converted to Roth within your TSP account.

The TSP elective deferral limit is $23,500. This limit applies to the combined total of traditional and Roth contributions. For uniformed services members, this does not apply to traditional contributions from combat-zone pay. The annual additions limit is $70,000. The annual additions limit is the total amount of all the contributions you make in a calendar year. This limit is per employer and includes money from all sources: employee contributions (tax-deferred, after-tax, and tax-exempt), Agency/Service Automatic (1%) Contributions, and Agency/Service Matching Contributions. It does not include catch-up contributions. The annual additions limit affects mostly members of the uniformed services who can exceed the annual elective deferral limit. The excess contributions go into the traditional portion of your account from tax-exempt pay earned in a combat zone.
